stem: One group of \( [[w1]] \) numbers has mean \( [[m1]] \), and a second group of \( [[w2]] \) numbers has mean \( [[m2]] \). What is the mean of all \( [[w1]] + [[w2]] \) numbers combined?
solution: The combined mean is the total of all values divided by the total count: \( \frac{[[w1]]\cdot[[m1]] + [[w2]]\cdot[[m2]]}{[[w1]] + [[w2]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the plain average of the two group means; it ignores that the groups have different sizes.
This adds the two group totals but forgets to divide by the combined count.
This swaps the two group sizes as weights, pairing each mean with the wrong count.
This is off by one from the correct combined mean.

stem: A set of \( [[n]] \) numbers has mean \( [[m]] \). A new value, \( [[v]] \), is added to the set. What is the mean of the resulting \( [[n]] + 1 \) numbers?
solution: The new total is \( [[n]]\cdot[[m]] + [[v]] \), spread over \( [[n]]+1 \) numbers: \( \frac{[[n]]\cdot[[m]] + [[v]]}{[[n]]+1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the plain average of the old mean and the new value, ignoring how many numbers were already in the set.
This adds the new value to the old total but forgets to divide by the new count.
This divides by the OLD count \( [[n]] \) instead of the new count \( [[n]]+1 \).
This assumes adding one value never changes the mean.
This is off by one from the correct new mean.

stem: A set of \( [[n]] \) numbers has mean \( [[m]] \). One value, \( [[v]] \), is removed from the set. What is the mean of the remaining \( [[n]] - 1 \) numbers?
solution: The remaining total is \( [[n]]\cdot[[m]] - [[v]] \), spread over \( [[n]]-1 \) numbers: \( \frac{[[n]]\cdot[[m]] - [[v]]}{[[n]]-1} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This still divides by the OLD count \( [[n]] \) instead of the new count \( [[n]]-1 \).
This is the remaining total without dividing by the new count at all.
This has the wrong sign on the subtraction, negating the whole result.
This assumes removing one value never changes the mean.
This is off by one from the correct new mean.

stem: A group of \( [[w1]] \) numbers has mean \( [[m1]] \). When combined with a second group of \( [[w2]] \) numbers, the mean of all \( [[w1]] + [[w2]] \) numbers together is \( [[M]] \). What is the mean of the second group?
solution: The combined total is \( [[M]]\cdot([[w1]]+[[w2]]) \), and the first group already contributes \( [[w1]]\cdot[[m1]] \), so the second group's total is \( [[M]]([[w1]]+[[w2]]) - [[w1]][[m1]] \); dividing by \( [[w2]] \) gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the second group has the same mean as the combined group.
This uses only \( [[w2]] \) (not the full combined count) when computing the combined total.
This swaps the two group sizes, dividing by \( [[w1]] \) instead of \( [[w2]] \).
This is the 'reflection' trick \( 2M-m_1 \), which only works when the group sizes are equal.
This is off by one from the correct mean.

stem: A set of \( [[n]] \) numbers has mean \( [[m]] \). After one new value is added, the mean of the resulting \( [[n]] + 1 \) numbers becomes \( [[mnew]] \). What value was added?
solution: The new total is \( ([[n]]+1)\cdot[[mnew]] \) and the old total was \( [[n]]\cdot[[m]] \), so the added value is the difference: \( ([[n]]+1)[[mnew]] - [[n]][[m]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the added value simply equals the new mean.
This swaps which mean gets multiplied by \( [[n]]+1\) versus \( [[n]] \).
This is just the change in the mean, not the value that produced that change.
This has the wrong sign, giving the negative of the correct added value.
This is off by one from the correct added value.
